name: llm-folder-index-entry-point
hook: src/infra/llm/index.ts is the folder-level entry point
body:
Added a folder-level @ai-summary to src/infra/llm/index.ts documenting it as the facade entry point. The @ai-summary captures the load-bearing gotchas: provider detection is per-request (not startup) and the error classifier mapping is the single point of failure for error classification.

**Why:** AI agents reading this folder first need to know the entry point and the key abstraction. The folder contains both public API and internal implementation; the index.ts is the canonical entry.

**How to apply:** When adding new AI services, export them from this index rather than from sub-folders to maintain the facade pattern.

name: ai-summary-pattern-never-restate
hook: @ai-summary captures why and the trap, never what code says
body:
The @ai-summary tag should capture: (1) why the module exists, (2) the load-bearing constraint or gotcha that could silently break if misunderstood. It should NOT restate what the code does — well-named functions already convey that. Examples:

**Good:** "Rate-limit errors are excluded from circuit-breaker failure counting — a single 429 would otherwise open the circuit when the provider is healthy."

**Bad:** "Retries with exponential backoff."

**Why:** AI agents reading this codebase need to understand load-bearing constraints, not implementations. Restating what code does adds no value over reading the code itself.

name: fix-duplicate-comment-closures
hook: When replacing a doc comment block, ensure no duplicate */ remains
body:
When replacing an existing `/** ... */` doc comment, be careful that the old comment's closing ` */` is not left behind after the replacement. The original module-level comments had trailing ` *` lines before ` */`; my replacements sometimes included an extra ` */` on its own line.

**Why:** TypeScript parser treats the first `*/` as the closing delimiter; a second `*/` on the next line causes a cascade of "Unexpected token" errors starting at the second line of the file.

**How to apply:** When editing existing doc blocks, read the full original block first and replace the entire block, not just the first line.
